title.title: Permissioned Blockchains: Hyperledger Fabric
title.subtitle: Enterprise blockchain design, supply chains, channels, and transaction flow

why.heading: Why Permissioned Blockchains?
why.subtitle: Public chains fall short for enterprise — here is why permissioned networks exist.

problems.label: Privacy
problems.desc: Transaction data is visible to all competitors on public networks

problems.label: Compliance
problems.desc: GDPR, HIPAA, KYC/AML regulations require controlled data access

problems.label: Performance
problems.desc: 7–15 TPS on public chains cannot handle enterprise transaction volumes

problems.label: Identity
problems.desc: Anonymous addresses are not legal entities — accountability is impossible

solutions.label: Known participants
solutions.desc: All members are verified with legal accountability — no anonymity

solutions.label: Configurable privacy
solutions.desc: Channels isolate sensitive data so only relevant parties can see it

solutions.label: High throughput
solutions.desc: No mining required — 3,000+ TPS is achievable with Raft consensus

supplychains.heading: Supply Chains: The Perfect Use Case
supplychains.subtitle: Multi-party data sharing with no single point of trust — exactly what blockchain solves.

deployments.label: Walmart × IBM Food Trust
deployments.detail: Live since 2018 — pork, leafy greens, mangoes · 100+ suppliers

deployments.label: Maersk TradeLens
deployments.detail: ⚠ Shut down 2022 — network-effect lesson, not tech failure

steps.label: Farmer
steps.preIssue: No timestamps · easy to forge · lost in transit

steps.label: Processor
steps.preIssue: Re-keyed data · dropped fields · version drift

steps.label: Distributor
steps.preIssue: Internal codes don’t match upstream IDs

steps.label: Consumer
steps.preIssue: No origin info · must trust labels

supplychains.siloedNote: Each party hoards data in its own system
supplychains.walmartCaseBody: tracing one contaminated mango required phoning 7 different systems and took ~7 days. Most of that was reconciling formats, not searching.
supplychains.fabricNote: Every step writes to one shared, append-only ledger

overview.title: Hyperledger Fabric
overview.description: An enterprise-grade permissioned blockchain framework by the Linux Foundation. Modular, private, and designed for business networks.

cards.title: Linux Foundation
cards.desc: Open source, governed by a consortium: IBM, Intel, SAP and many more.

cards.title: Modular Architecture
cards.desc: Swap consensus, membership services, and storage independently.

cards.title: Identity-First
cards.desc: Every participant holds a verified certificate issued by an MSP/CA.

keyPoints: No mining — consensus by designated orderer nodes
Chaincode (smart contracts) written in Go, JavaScript, or Java
Channels create private sub-networks within the same Fabric network
Used by IBM Food Trust, TradeLens, HSBC, Maersk, Walmart

fabricx.heading: Fabric Architecture Deep Dive
fabricx.subtitle: The five components and how they connect inside a single channel.

inOneLine.e: whose transactions are sequenced by an external

components.label: Organizations
components.desc: Member companies — each with their own peers and MSP.

components.label: Peer Nodes
components.desc: Hold ledger copies, execute chaincode, and endorse transactions.

components.label: Orderer
components.desc: Creates block order via Raft/BFT — the consensus service.

components.label: MSP / CA
components.desc: Certificate Authority — issues identities, proves who you are.

components.label: Ledger
components.desc: World State DB + blockchain log — the persistent record.

channelFooter.a: Every peer in the channel holds an
channelFooter.c: . Each org's MSP issues its own identities; cross-org identities are validated via the channel's trust roots.

fabricx.chaincodeNoteBody: Go / JavaScript / Java programs deployed to peers. Executed during endorsement; their read/write set is signed and forwarded to the orderer for sequencing.

consensus.heading: Pluggable Consensus
consensus.subtitle: Different enterprise environments need different trust models — Fabric adapts.
consensus.whyBody: A bank consortium with fully trusted members has very different needs from a supply chain with competing companies. Fabric's pluggable orderer means you pick the right tool.

options.label: Raft
options.desc: Crash Fault Tolerant. Simple leader-follower model — ideal for trusted, known environments.

options.label: SmartBFT
options.desc: Byzantine Fault Tolerant. Handles malicious nodes — heavier but necessary in hostile networks.

options.label: Custom
options.desc: An open interface allows future consensus algorithms to be plugged in without rebuilding.

bft.heading: Byzantine Fault Tolerance in Fabric
bft.subtitle: How distributed systems survive malicious — not just crashed — participants.
bft.defLabel: The Byzantine Generals Problem · Lamport, Shostak & Pease, 1982

def.b: if it stays correct even when up to

formulaNote.b: twice — once to detect lying, once to converge. If

bft.faultLookLabel: What does a Byzantine fault look like?

equivocationNote.body: — one node sends conflicting facts to different peers. CFT (Raft) cannot detect this; BFT can.

scenarios.label: Competitor on the network
scenarios.desc: Two rivals sharing one ledger. BFT prevents either from feeding manipulated data into shared state.

scenarios.label: Compromised peer
scenarios.desc: A hacked node sending different read/write sets to different validators is detected and outvoted.

scenarios.label: Regulatory mandate
scenarios.desc: Finance, defense, and cross-jurisdiction networks often require BFT guarantees by compliance.

raftStrip.b: . Trusts orderer operators not to lie. Lighter, faster, simpler — fits most consortiums.

bftStrip.b: . Required when operators are competitors, regulated, or untrusted at the protocol level.

channels.heading: Channels: Selective Data Sharing
channels.subtitle: Four companies on one Fabric network — three channels, each with different membership and data. Click a channel to see who can read it.

channels.label: Joint R&D
channels.data: Shared drug research, trial data, patents
channels.why: Competitors collaborate on R&D but keep results hidden from hospitals and regulators until ready.

channels.label: Drug Supply
channels.why: Supply chain between one pharma and one hospital — PharmaB should not see their competitor's sales volumes.

channels.label: Compliance
channels.data: Audit logs, regulatory filings, adverse event reports
channels.why: Regulator needs visibility into safety and compliance across the whole network.

channels.emptyHint: ← Click a channel to see who has access

txflow.heading: Transaction Flow in Fabric
txflow.subtitle: Five stages from proposal to confirmation — execute-order-validate model.

steps.label: Propose
steps.desc: Client sends a transaction proposal to the designated endorsing peers.

steps.label: Endorse
steps.desc: Peers simulate chaincode execution and return a signed endorsement. No ledger write yet.

steps.label: Order
steps.desc: Client submits the endorsed transaction to the Orderer. Orderer batches transactions into a block.

steps.label: Validate & Commit
steps.desc: All peers validate endorsement signatures and commit the block to their ledger copy.

steps.label: Notify
steps.desc: Events are emitted to the client confirming the transaction has been committed.

txflow.eovBodyB: ordering. This means multiple transactions can be simulated in parallel by different peers simultaneously — unlocking high throughput.
txflow.oeBody: Traditional blockchains (Ethereum) order first, then all nodes execute sequentially. This creates a bottleneck. Fabric's approach avoids this entirely.
txflow.resultBody: 3,000+ TPS achievable without sacrificing Byzantine resilience when SmartBFT is enabled.

consensusEvo.heading: Consensus Evolution in Fabric
consensusEvo.subtitle: Fabric's ordering layer evolved in three major steps — each removing the previous version's biggest constraint.

versions.name: Apache Kafka
versions.desc: External Kafka cluster handled block ordering, with Zookeeper for coordination. Reliable but operationally heavy — a non-blockchain dependency that consortiums had to provision and patch separately.
versions.limit: Not BFT · Kafka + ZK = a central operational chokepoint outside the blockchain layer.
versions.solved: First stable ordering after the v0.6 PBFT prototype — proved Fabric's execute-order-validate model worked at scale.
versions.adoption: Removed entirely in Fabric v2.5 (2021). Legacy networks only.

versions.name: Raft (CFT)
versions.desc: Built-in Crash Fault Tolerant consensus. Orderer nodes elect a leader; the leader replicates an append-only log of blocks to followers. Simple, fast, and requires zero external infrastructure.
versions.limit: Assumes no malicious orderers — tolerates crashes, not Byzantine behavior.
versions.solved: Eliminated the Kafka + Zookeeper dependency · single binary deployment.
versions.adoption: Used by virtually every production Fabric consortium today (IBM Food Trust, we.trade, TradeLens, government registries).

versions.name: SmartBFT
versions.desc: Byzantine Fault Tolerant ordering service. Handles both crashes AND malicious orderer nodes. Designed for adversarial consortiums where parties don't trust each other to operate orderers honestly.
versions.limit: Heavier than Raft · 3-phase consensus · need at least 4 orderers to tolerate 1 malicious one.
versions.solved: Removes the trust-the-orderer-operator assumption — enables truly adversarial enterprise networks.
versions.adoption: Picked when orderer operators are competitors or regulated entities (cross-bank settlement, defense, multi-jurisdiction).

decisionBft.b: . Pay the throughput cost for true Byzantine resilience.

raft.heading: Raft: How It Works
raft.subtitle: Raft breaks consensus into three phases — click each to explore.

phases.label: Leader Election
phases.desc: On startup or after a leader crash, nodes become Candidates and request votes. The first to reach a majority becomes the new Leader.
details: Each node starts as a Follower
If no heartbeat from leader → becomes Candidate
Candidate sends RequestVote to all peers
First to get ⌊N/2⌋+1 votes wins the election
Term counter increments with every election

phases.label: Log Replication
phases.desc: The Leader receives all client requests and appends them to its log. It then replicates this entry to all Followers before acknowledging the client.
details: Client sends transaction to Leader only
Leader appends entry to its own log
Leader sends AppendEntries RPC to all Followers
Followers append to their own logs and reply

phases.label: Commit
phases.desc: Once a majority of followers have written the entry, the Leader marks it as committed and notifies all followers. The entry is now permanent.
details: Leader commits the entry in its state machine
Leader broadcasts commit in next AppendEntries

raft.quorumNote: 5 nodes → needs 3 votes · 3 nodes → needs 2 votes · Tolerates ⌊(N−1)/2⌋ failures

exerciseSupply.heading: Exercise: What Goes On Chain?
exerciseSupply.subtitle: Walmart wants to trace lettuce from farm to store on Hyperledger Fabric. For each event, decide: should it be recorded on the blockchain?

events.label: Batch ID assigned at harvest
events.context: A farm assigns a unique batch number to a crate of lettuce before it leaves the field.
events.reason: Immutable origin record — enables full provenance traceability from field to store. This is the anchor for the entire chain.

events.label: Farmer negotiates price with processor
events.context: The farm owner and the processing plant agree on a price per kilogram over email.
events.reason: Commercial negotiation is private and off-chain. Pricing data is competitive information and does not require multi-party immutability.

events.label: Temperature logged during refrigerated transport
events.context: IoT sensors record temperature every 30 minutes inside the refrigerated truck.
events.reason: Cold-chain integrity evidence is critical for food safety and liability. Any tampering with this data could conceal contamination.

events.label: Processing plant quality inspection passed
events.context: A third-party inspector certifies the batch meets food safety standards.
events.reason: Certification of compliance must be tamper-proof and instantly auditable by retailers and regulators — a perfect blockchain use case.

events.label: Retail price set by supermarket
events.context: The supermarket updates its POS system with the sale price for the product.
events.reason: Pricing is a unilateral commercial decision. Competitors on the same supply network should not see each other's retail margins.

events.label: Product recalled due to contamination alert
events.context: A contamination is detected. A recall notice needs to reach all supply chain partners immediately.
events.reason: Recall events must be immutable, timestamped, and instantly visible across all participants — blockchain enables 2.2-second full-chain alerts (Walmart case).

events.label: Distributor updates internal ERP system
events.context: The distributor enters the delivery into their own SAP system after the truck arrives.
events.reason: Internal ERP updates are siloed operations. They do not require multi-party verification and may contain sensitive business data.

events.label: Consumer scans QR code to verify product origin
events.context: A shopper at the store scans the product QR code on their phone.
events.reason: Reading from the ledger is always valid. The blockchain provides a public-facing provenance window while writing remains controlled.

exerciseHealth.heading: Exercise: Design the Channel Structure
exerciseHealth.subtitle: A hospital network is deploying Hyperledger Fabric for private health data. Assign each data type to the correct channel.
exerciseHealth.subtitleHint: Select a card, then click a channel.

channels.label: Billing Channel
channels.desc: Financial claims and reimbursements. No clinical detail.

channels.label: Compliance Channel
channels.desc: Audit logs and regulatory reporting only.

production.heading: Fabric in production — three honest case studies
production.subtitle: What real Fabric networks look like at scale — including one that succeeded, one that shut down, and one quietly becoming critical infrastructure.

cases.name: IBM Food Trust
cases.members: Walmart · Carrefour · Nestlé · Tyson · Unilever · Albertsons · 100+ orgs
cases.flow: Harvest → processing → shipping → retail. Each step writes provenance + cold-chain data to the channel.
cases.outcome: Walmart mango trace: 7 days → 2.2 sec. Used live in salmonella outbreak responses. Walmart now requires Food Trust onboarding for leafy-green suppliers.
cases.lesson: Worked because (a) Walmart could mandate adoption, and (b) the alternative was decades-old paper / EDI workflows that genuinely failed during recalls.

cases.name: Maersk TradeLens
cases.members: Maersk + IBM · select carriers · 50+ ports · 200M+ events / yr at peak
cases.flow: Shipping events (load, unload, customs, transhipment) written to the channel by carriers, ports, and customs authorities.
cases.outcome: Reduced docs processing time. But: rival carriers (MSC, CMA CGM, Hapag-Lloyd) refused to join a Maersk-branded network. Network effects collapsed. Both partners exited Q1 2022.
cases.lesson: Permissioned blockchain needs neutral governance OR a regulator mandate. A consortium owned by one competitor is a contradiction in terms — others won't feed data to a rival's ledger.

cases.name: EU EBSI
cases.members: 27 EU member states + EEA · cross-border verifiable credentials · part of EU Digital Identity Wallet
cases.flow: Issues and verifies cross-border credentials: diplomas, professional licences, eIDAS attestations, customs documents — backed by member-state validators.
cases.outcome: Becomes mandatory infrastructure for the EU Digital Identity Wallet (eIDAS 2.0, member-state mandate by 2026). Universities issue Fabric-anchored diplomas verifiable across borders.
cases.lesson: Government-led consortiums sidestep the network-effect problem — participation is a regulatory obligation, not a market choice. The most boring Fabric deployments may end up the most consequential.

production.patternBody: Permissioned blockchain succeeds where (a) a credible authority can require participation (Walmart's market power, the EU's regulatory mandate), and (b) the alternative process — paper, EDI, faxes, siloed ERP — has been demonstrably broken for years. Where the alternative is "good enough Excel", consortiums fail quietly.

bestfits.heading: Where Hyperledger Fabric Wins — The Use Cases It Actually Owns

intro.a: Fabric's design choices (permissioned membership, channels for privacy, pluggable consensus, no native token) make it the default whenever the participants are
intro.strong: known organisations who need to share a ledger without sharing all their data publicly

cases.name: Multi-party supply chain provenance
cases.why: Every step (manufacturer → distributor → retailer) is recorded; channels keep commercial pricing private between specific pairs.
cases.example: IBM Food Trust (Walmart, Carrefour, Nestlé) · Maersk TradeLens (deprecated but proof of pattern) · GSBN ocean shipping.

cases.name: Inter-bank settlement & trade finance
cases.why: KYC-by-design, regulator-friendly, ordering service guarantees finality without PoW costs.
cases.example: we.trade · Marco Polo · Santander Onyx · Eurosystem TIPS pilots.

cases.name: Healthcare records & pharma serialisation
cases.why: Permissioned access + channels match HIPAA/GDPR requirements. Drug pedigree provable without exposing patient data.
cases.example: MediLedger (US drug serialisation) · Change Healthcare claims · regional patient-record exchanges.

cases.name: Identity & credential consortia
cases.why: No anonymous participants — every party signs with a credential issued by a trusted MSP. Fits regulated identity frameworks.
cases.example: Sovrin-style verifiable credentials · government B2B portals · CBDC interbank rails (pilots).

bestfits.notAFitBody: permissionless DeFi (use Ethereum), digital scarcity / SoV (use Bitcoin), consumer apps requiring open self-custody, anything where censorship-resistance against the consortium itself is a requirement.

worstfits.intro: Fabric's permissioned, no-token, channel-based design is brilliant for B2B consortia — and almost useless for anything that needs an open, public, value-bearing network.

cases.name: Public consumer apps / dApps
cases.why: Every participant needs an MSP-issued identity. No anonymous users, no permissionless onboarding — incompatible with the public-internet UX.
cases.alt: Use Ethereum (+ L2s) · Solana · Sui for open consumer apps and wallet-based onboarding.

cases.name: Censorship-resistant value / SoV
cases.why: A consortium can vote to remove members, freeze assets, or change rules. By design — and exactly what you don't want for neutral money.
cases.alt: Use Bitcoin for trust-minimised, jurisdiction-neutral value storage.

cases.name: Composable DeFi / liquidity
cases.why: No native token, no open liquidity pools, no shared accounts across organisations. DeFi composability requires permissionless writers + a public AMM.
cases.alt: Use Ethereum + L2s · Solana · Sui · Cosmos for DeFi composability and shared liquidity.

cases.name: NFT marketplaces / open creator economy
cases.why: No global token standards (ERC-721/1155 don't exist), no public discoverability, no anonymous buyer/seller pairings. Cultural fit is wrong.
cases.alt: Use Ethereum · Polygon · Solana · Base for NFTs, marketplaces, royalties.

rightToolBody.d: — supply chain consortia, inter-bank rails, healthcare exchanges. For everything else, pick a public chain.

vocab.heading: Fabric ↔ Public Blockchain — Same Mechanism, Different Vocabulary

intro.a: Most of Fabric is the public-blockchain primitives you already know,
intro.b: . Read the right column once and Fabric stops feeling like a different planet.

rows.note: Your "account" in Fabric is a certificate issued by the Membership Service Provider (MSP) for your org. The CA replaces the public-key infra you do yourself in a wallet.

rows.fab: X.509 cert + private key in MSP
rows.note: Same crypto, different distribution. CA issues, MSP revokes. The wallet primitive moves into a controlled enterprise PKI.
A program that runs against the world state and emits state writes. Go / Node.js / Java instead of Solidity, but the role is identical.

rows.fab: Identity hash / MSP ID + cert subject
rows.note: A canonical pointer to "who you are" on the chain. Fabric attaches it to your org rather than to a self-sovereign key.
No native token in Fabric. You write a chaincode that tracks balances or asset records yourself, often using the official Token SDK template.
Fabric has no economic spam pressure — participants are vetted. Compute is paid for off-chain by the consortium that runs the network.
Same thing. The Orderer assembles transactions into blocks and broadcasts them to channel members.
Fabric splits the role: Endorsers simulate + sign tx proposals; Orderers (Raft/BFT cluster) put them in deterministic order. No PoW, no PoS.
Crash-fault-tolerant ordering by default. BFT ordering exists for stronger trust assumptions. No PoW.
Tx is first proposed to endorsers, then submitted to the orderer for sequencing. There is no global mempool — Fabric never had a fee market.
A channel is a self-contained ledger with its own members, chaincode, and history — completely invisible to non-members on the same network.
Members vote off-chain or via signed config transactions. Fabric never adopted on-chain token-vote governance.

vocab.bottomLineBody: Fabric is mostly the same primitives, packaged for a different buyer. Don't relearn cryptography — relearn the brand names. The pedagogical move is just to keep this glossary open the first few times you read a Fabric tutorial.

quiz.question: In Hyperledger Fabric, three pharmaceutical companies (PharmaA, PharmaB, and a Hospital) are on the same network. PharmaA and PharmaB want to share pricing data that the Hospital must NOT see. What Fabric feature enables this?

options.text: Pluggable consensus — PharmaA and PharmaB use a separate Raft ordering service that excludes the Hospital.
A private Channel — only PharmaA and PharmaB are members of this channel; the Hospital sees none of its transactions or ledger state.
A separate MSP (Membership Service Provider) — the Hospital is issued a different certificate type that limits its read access.
Zero-knowledge proofs — PharmaA and PharmaB submit proofs instead of raw data, hiding the pricing details from the Hospital.

quiz.explanation: Channels are Hyperledger Fabric's primary privacy mechanism. A Channel is a private sub-ledger with its own set of member organizations, chaincode, and transaction history — invisible to non-members on the same network. PharmaA and PharmaB create a channel that only they join; the Hospital cannot see its transactions, state, or even that it exists. This is fundamentally different from public blockchains where all data is visible to all participants. MSPs control identity and enrollment, not data visibility between enrolled members.

quiz2.question: In a Fabric consortium between BankA and BankB, every peer must prove its identity when submitting a proposal or endorsement. Which Fabric component issues and validates these identities?

options.text: The Ordering Service — Raft nodes verify each transaction's identity before sequencing it into a block.

The Membership Service Provider (MSP) — each organization runs its own MSP backed by a Certificate Authority that issues X.509 certificates to peers, clients and admins; remote peers validate signatures by trusting the issuer's root CA.
The Channel Configuration Block — a static list of public keys committed at channel genesis is checked on every transaction.
The Smart Contract (chaincode) — each chaincode must include identity-validation logic in its Init function.

quiz2.explanation: Fabric is permissioned by design and relies on PKI. Every organization runs an MSP that maps cryptographic material (X.509 certificates issued by its CA) to roles (peer / client / admin / orderer). When a peer signs a proposal or endorsement, peers from other orgs validate the signature against the configured root certificate of the signer's MSP — listed in the channel configuration. The ordering service does NOT validate business identity; it only orders transactions that have already been endorsed. Identities are configured per-channel via the channel config, not hardcoded in chaincode, so adding a new org is a configuration change, not a redeployment.

quiz3.question: A supply-chain chaincode is deployed to a channel with three orgs (Producer, Distributor, Retailer) and an endorsement policy of AND('Producer.peer', 'Distributor.peer'). The Retailer submits a transaction proposal that updates a delivery record. What is required for this transaction to be committed to the ledger?

options.text: A single Retailer peer endorses the proposal — the Retailer is the submitter, so its endorsement is sufficient.
The proposal must be endorsed by at least one peer from Producer AND at least one peer from Distributor before reaching the orderer; the Retailer's endorsement is irrelevant to the policy.
All three orgs (Producer, Distributor, Retailer) must endorse — Fabric defaults to unanimous endorsement when the submitter is on the channel.
The Raft orderer endorses on behalf of any unspecified org once a quorum of orderer nodes is reached.

quiz3.explanation: Endorsement policy is enforced independently of the submitter. The client (Retailer here) sends the proposal directly to the peers listed in the policy — Producer and Distributor — collects their signed read/write sets, and only then forwards the endorsed transaction to the ordering service. The orderer batches and orders transactions but never endorses. Committing peers verify on receipt that the collected endorsements satisfy the policy before applying the state change; if the policy isn't met, the transaction is marked invalid in the block but still recorded (so the audit trail captures every attempt). Submitter identity gives no special endorsement power.

takeaways.title: Section 03 — Key Takeaways
items: Permissioned blockchains exist for regulated, multi-party environments where participants are known
Hyperledger Fabric is the leading enterprise blockchain — modular, flexible, and production-proven
Channels allow different subsets of participants to share private data on the same network
Smart contracts (chaincode) in Fabric automate business logic across supply chain partners
Pluggable consensus means Fabric can swap its ordering mechanism without rebuilding the network
BFT consensus tolerates Byzantine (malicious) failures — essential in adversarial environments
